one_liner: A 42-year-old man is brought to clinic by his wife for 18 months of involuntary jerky movements, personality changes, and worsening irritability. His father died in a psychiatric facility at age 55 with a similar illness.

stage2.hpi_extension: The wife reports that over the past year and a half her husband has developed brief, irregular, dance-like movements of his face, hands, and shoulders that he cannot suppress. He has become irritable, impulsive, and socially inappropriate, lost his accounting job after making repeated errors, and admits to low mood with passive suicidal thoughts. His memory and executive function have declined. His father developed similar movements and psychiatric symptoms in his late 40s and died in a psychiatric facility at 55; a paternal uncle had a similar illness. The patient has two children, ages 12 and 15.
stage2.pmh: Depression treated with sertraline for 2 years
stage2.family_history: Father and paternal uncle with progressive movement disorder and dementia beginning in their late 40s; paternal grandmother reportedly 'nervous' and died young
stage2.social_history: Married, two children. Former accountant, recently dismissed. Minimal alcohol, no drugs.
stage2.physical_exam: Mental status: oriented but with poor attention, impaired serial 7s, and blunted affect. Continuous, irregular, non-stereotyped jerking movements of the fingers, face, and tongue (chorea). Motor impersistence (cannot keep tongue protruded or sustain hand grip — 'milkmaid's grip'). Cranial nerves grossly intact. Strength preserved. Reflexes 2+ and symmetric. Gait wide-based with superimposed choreiform movements. Cerebellar testing normal.

lab_results.result: HTT (huntingtin) gene analysis on chromosome 4p16.3: CAG trinucleotide repeat expansion of 46 on one allele (normal <27; 27-35 intermediate; 36-39 reduced penetrance; >=40 full penetrance). Confirms Huntington disease.
lab_results.clinical_significance: CAG repeat expansion in the HTT gene is diagnostic of Huntington disease. Longer repeats correlate with earlier onset, and the repeat number tends to expand further when transmitted through the paternal germline, explaining anticipation within families.

lab_results.result: MRI brain: marked atrophy of the caudate nuclei bilaterally with enlargement (flattening) of the frontal horns of the lateral ventricles ('box-car' ventricles). Mild generalized cortical atrophy. No focal lesion.
lab_results.clinical_significance: Caudate atrophy with 'box-car' ventricles is the classic imaging finding in Huntington disease, reflecting selective loss of medium spiny neurons in the striatum.

stage5.final_clue: Genetic counseling is arranged for the patient's children. Tetrabenazine (a vesicular monoamine transporter inhibitor) is started to reduce chorea, and his depression is optimized. Family is counseled about the autosomal dominant inheritance and ~50% risk for each child.

debrief.classic_presentation: Huntington disease classically presents in the 30s-50s (mean ~40) with a triad of chorea (brief, jerky, dance-like involuntary movements), psychiatric disturbance (depression, irritability, impulsivity, psychosis), and progressive dementia. Motor impersistence ('milkmaid's grip', inability to keep the tongue protruded) and oculomotor abnormalities appear early. The family history is usually positive because of autosomal dominant inheritance. Death typically occurs 15-20 years after symptom onset, often from aspiration pneumonia or suicide. Juvenile-onset (Westphal variant) presents before age 20 with parkinsonism and seizures and is associated with very large repeats, usually paternally inherited.
debrief.pathophysiology: Huntington disease is caused by an expanded CAG trinucleotide repeat (>=36, classically >=40 for full penetrance) in the HTT (huntingtin) gene on chromosome 4p16.3. The expanded polyglutamine tract produces a toxic gain-of-function protein that aggregates and causes selective degeneration of GABAergic medium spiny neurons in the caudate and putamen (striatum), leading to loss of inhibition of the thalamus and resulting hyperkinetic movements. Neurotransmitter findings: decreased GABA and ACh in the striatum; relative dopamine excess. Anticipation — earlier onset and more severe disease in successive generations — is prominent, especially with paternal transmission, due to further expansion of the repeat during spermatogenesis.
debrief.treatment_overview: No disease-modifying therapy exists. Chorea can be suppressed with tetrabenazine or deutetrabenazine (VMAT2 inhibitors that deplete presynaptic dopamine) or with typical/atypical antipsychotics (haloperidol, risperidone). Psychiatric symptoms are treated with SSRIs and antipsychotics as needed. Genetic counseling and predictive testing should be offered to at-risk family members. Supportive care including speech, physical, and occupational therapy, and eventually enteral nutrition as dysphagia develops.
high_yield_pearls: Huntington disease = autosomal dominant CAG trinucleotide repeat expansion in the HTT gene on chromosome 4 (think 'Hunting 4 food').
Classic triad: chorea + dementia + psychiatric disturbance, onset in the 30s-50s.
MRI shows caudate atrophy with 'box-car' (flat-bottomed) lateral ventricles; pathology shows loss of GABAergic medium spiny neurons in the striatum.
Anticipation: earlier onset with successive generations due to further CAG expansion, particularly through paternal transmission.
Treat chorea with tetrabenazine (VMAT2 inhibitor); there is no cure — median survival ~15-20 years from onset.
sources: First Aid for the USMLE Step 1 (2017) — Neurology and Special Senses chapter, 'Movement disorders — Huntington disease' section.
First Aid for the USMLE Step 1 (2017) — Biochemistry chapter, 'Trinucleotide repeat expansion diseases' section.
